name: Gusto
tagline: Payroll, benefits, and HR for US-based small businesses who want automated tax filing and easy onboarding
description: Gusto is a modern payroll, benefits, and HR platform designed for US-based small businesses that want to handle employee administration without complexity. It automates payroll processing, tax filings, benefits administration, and new hire onboarding in one integrated system. With over 300,000+ businesses using Gusto, it has become the leading payroll-first HR platform for US-based small companies.
pros: Fully automated payroll with tax filings calculated, filed, and paid automatically on every payroll run with zero manual intervention
New hire onboarding that auto-populates federal and state tax forms, sends e-signature requests, and creates employee accounts
Health insurance administration with multi-carrier comparison shopping, enrollment management, and ACA compliance reporting
Generous partner ecosystem with native integrations to QuickBooks, Xero, and major time-tracking and accounting tools
Employee self-service portal with an intuitive mobile app where team members can view pay stubs, W-2s, and PTO balances without contacting HR
cons: Limited to US customers only with no international payroll support, excluding growing remote teams with global hires
Time tracking and PTO management features feel basic compared to dedicated HR platforms like BambooHR
Higher per-employee pricing than competitors once you exceed 25 employees, making it expensive for growing companies

features.name: Automated Payroll
features.description: Full-cycle payroll processing with automatic calculations for wages, deductions, and employer taxes across all states

features.name: Tax Filing
features.description: Federal, state, and local tax filings calculated, filed, and paid automatically on schedule with guaranteed accuracy

features.name: Direct Deposit
features.description: Next-day or same-day direct deposit to employee bank accounts with detailed pay stub breakdowns and pay history

features.name: Employee Self-Service
features.description: Employee portal for viewing pay stubs, W-2s, PTO balances, benefit elections, and updating personal information

features.name: New Hire Onboarding
features.description: Guided digital onboarding that collects W-4, I-9, and state tax forms with e-signatures and automatic account creation

features.name: Health Benefits Administration
features.description: Medical, dental, and vision plan administration with carrier integrations, enrollment management, and premium deduction handling

features.name: 401(k) Management
features.description: Retirement plan setup and administration with automated payroll deductions, employer matching, and compliance testing

features.name: Workers' Comp Admin
features.description: Workers' compensation insurance administration with pay-as-you-go premium calculation and audit protection

features.name: PTO Management
features.description: Configurable paid time off policies with accrual tracking, employee requests, manager approval workflows, and balance reporting

features.name: Time Tracking
features.description: Basic time clock functionality with GPS location, overtime calculation, and direct integration with payroll processing

features.name: Employee Surveys
features.description: Anonymous employee engagement surveys with template questions and aggregate reporting for culture insights

features.name: HR Reporting
features.description: Customizable reports for payroll summaries, tax liabilities, headcount, turnover, compensation, and compliance audits

features.name: Compliance Documents
features.description: Automated generation and distribution of W-2s, 1095-Cs, 1099-NECs, and other mandatory compliance forms

features.name: Integrations (QuickBooks, Xero)
features.description: Two-way sync with QuickBooks Online and Xero for accurate bookkeeping, reconciliation, and journal entry automation

features.name: Mobile Payroll App
features.description: iOS and Android app for running payroll, approving time-off requests, and viewing employee information on the go

features.name: Contractor Management
features.description: 1099 contractor onboarding, payment processing, and year-end tax form generation with separate contractor portal

features.name: Garnishment Management
features.description: Automated processing of wage garnishments including child support, tax levies, and student loan deductions

features.name: Hire/Onboard API
features.description: API endpoints for programmatic employee onboarding, data synchronization, and custom integration development

content.title: Executive Summary
content.body: Gusto is the leading payroll-first HR platform for US-based small businesses, serving over 300,000 companies with an all-in-one solution for payroll processing, tax filing, benefits administration, and employee onboarding. Unlike traditional payroll providers ADP and Paychex, which layer consumer-friendly interfaces on top of decades-old mainframe systems, Gusto was built from the ground up for the small business owner who handles HR alongside sales, product development, and customer support. The platform automates the entire payroll cycle: calculating wages, deductions, and employer taxes; filing federal, state, and local taxes on schedule; and disbursing direct deposits to employees. Beyond payroll, Gusto handles health benefits administration with multi-carrier comparison shopping, 401(k) management, workers' compensation insurance, new hire onboarding with digital I-9 and W-4 forms, and PTO tracking. Gusto's distinct advantage is its consumer-grade user experience — both administrators and employees report finding the interface intuitive and pleasant, which is notably unusual in the payroll and HR software category. The company is SOC 2 compliant with bank-level encryption, and its tax filing accuracy guarantee (they pay penalties for errors) provides peace of mind for business owners who dread payroll tax compliance.

content.title: Best For
content.body: Gusto is best suited for US-based small businesses with 1 to 100 employees that want to handle payroll, benefits, and HR compliance without hiring dedicated HR staff or learning complex payroll systems. It is especially valuable for founders, office managers, and operations leads who manage HR as one of many responsibilities — the platform's guided workflows and transparent pricing reduce the anxiety associated with payroll tax compliance. Companies with 5-25 employees realize the strongest ROI because Gusto's per-person pricing is competitive at that scale, and the time savings from automated tax filing and onboarding provide measurable productivity gains. Businesses that need health benefits administration alongside payroll benefit from Gusto's integrated benefits marketplace, which allows employers to shop for medical, dental, and vision plans from multiple carriers directly within the platform and manage enrollments alongside payroll deductions. Companies with contractors (1099 workers) benefit from Gusto's contractor management features, which handle invoicing, payment processing, and year-end tax form generation separately from employee payroll.

content.title: Worst Fit
content.body: Gusto is a poor fit for three scenarios. Companies with employees outside the United States cannot use Gusto because the platform only supports US payroll, tax filing, and compliance. Growing companies that hire international contractors or remote employees abroad must maintain a separate solution for those workers, adding administrative complexity and cost. Organizations with more than 100 employees will find Gusto's per-person pricing becomes significantly more expensive than enterprise competitors like ADP, Paychex, or Rippling — at 150 employees, Gusto costs approximately $940/month plus $6/employee, while ADP can negotiate rates of $3-5/employee at that scale. Companies that need advanced HR capabilities beyond payroll and benefits — performance management, learning management systems, succession planning, or detailed workforce analytics — will find Gusto's HR features basic compared to dedicated platforms like BambooHR or Rippling. Gusto's time tracking and PTO management are functional but lack the sophistication of standalone time and attendance systems. Regulated industries with complex union reporting, prevailing wage calculations, or multi-state certified payroll requirements should evaluate specialized construction or healthcare payroll platforms.

content.title: Key Features
content.body: Gusto combines essential payroll and HR functionality into an impressively cohesive experience that prioritizes ease of use and compliance automation.
items: Automated tax filing handles all federal, state, and local payroll taxes — calculating, filing, and paying them on schedule with a 100% accuracy guarantee that covers any penalties or interest resulting from Gusto errors.

New hire onboarding walks employees through digital W-4, I-9, and direct deposit setup, auto-creates their accounts, and sends them welcome materials with company policies and benefit enrollment links.

Health benefits administration lets employers shop plans from multiple carriers, manage open enrollment with dependent verifications, and sync premium deductions directly to payroll without manual entry.

Contractor management supports 1099-NEC filing, milestone-based invoicing with payment processing, and a separate contractor portal for payment history and tax document access independent of employee records.

Integrations with QuickBooks, Xero, and major time-tracking apps ensure payroll data flows into accounting systems without duplicate entry, with mapped chart-of-accounts categories for clean reconciliation.
Employee self-service portal gives every team member access to pay stubs, W-2s, PTO balances, benefit documents, and a mobile app for viewing pay information on the go without contacting HR.

content.title: Real Advantages
content.body: Gusto's strongest advantage is its user experience quality — the interface is genuinely pleasant to use, which is a meaningful differentiator in the payroll and HR category where most products feel like they were designed in 1998 and never updated. New users can run their first payroll in under 30 minutes without training, and the platform guides administrators through every process with contextual help and clear error messages. The automated tax filing is Gusto's most consequential feature: it calculates, files, and pays all federal, state, and local payroll taxes on the required schedule, and handles quarterly and year-end filings (Form 941, SUTA, W-2s) without administrator intervention. Gusto's 100% tax accuracy guarantee means they pay any penalties or interest resulting from their errors — a significant risk transfer that provides genuine peace of mind for small business owners who fear IRS penalties. The new hire onboarding experience is another real advantage: employees receive a guided digital workflow that collects their W-4, I-9 (including remote document verification), and direct deposit information, auto-creates their Gusto account, and sends welcome materials — all without the employer printing, scanning, or mailing a single document. The onboarding flow takes the employee approximately 10-15 minutes and eliminates the 30-60 minutes of administrative work that manual onboarding requires per new hire.

content.title: Real Limitations
content.body: Gusto's most significant limitation is its US-only focus. The platform cannot process payroll for employees based outside the United States, and cannot handle the complex tax, social security, and employment law requirements of other countries. Companies with international remote employees must maintain a separate global payroll or Employer of Record (EOR) solution like Deel, Remote, or Rippling, creating a fragmented payroll admin experience. Gusto's time tracking and PTO management features are functional but basic — time tracking offers a simple clock-in/clock-out interface with GPS location but lacks advanced scheduling, shift swapping, labor cost forecasting, and compliance with state-specific meal and rest break rules. PTO management supports accrual policies and request workflows but does not have the configurable carryover rules, prorated accruals for mid-year hires, or blackout period management that larger organizations require. The per-person pricing becomes expensive at scale — Gusto charges $6/employee/month on all plans, while competitors like ADP, Paychex, and Rippling offer lower per-employee rates for businesses over 25 employees. A 50-employee company pays $300/month in per-person fees alone on Gusto, compared to approximately $150-200/month on Rippling or ADP at that size.

content.title: Pricing Explained
content.body: Gusto offers four pricing tiers. Simple at $40/month plus $6/person includes full-service payroll for employees and contractors, automated tax filings, employee self-service, health benefits administration, workers' compensation administration, and direct deposit. Plus at $60/month plus $6/person adds time tracking with GPS, PTO management, employee satisfaction surveys, and same-day direct deposit. Premium at $135/month plus $6/person adds a dedicated HR advisor, compliance alerts, workforce costing, and custom onboarding checklists — this plan is designed for companies with 25+ employees that need HR guidance beyond payroll processing. Contractor Only at $6/person/month provides contractor payments, 1099-NEC filing, and a contractor portal without employee payroll capabilities. All pricing is for monthly billing; annual billing provides a discount of approximately 10%. Gusto does not offer a free trial but provides a 30-day money-back guarantee on first payroll if customers are not satisfied. Setup and data migration are included at no additional cost, with Gusto's onboarding specialists handling the transfer of employee data, payroll history, and tax accounts from the previous provider.

content.title: Hidden Costs
content.body: Three hidden costs affect Gusto customers. The most common is the per-person fee ballooning with headcount — Gusto charges $6/employee/month regardless of whether they are active on payroll that pay period, so companies with seasonal workers, leaves of absence, or unpaid interns pay for every employee record in the system. A company with 40 employees but only 35 active on payroll any given month still pays $240/month in per-person fees. The second hidden cost is health benefits administration setup fees: while Gusto does not charge extra for benefits administration, the insurance carriers themselves charge setup and administration fees that are passed through to the employer. Small businesses (under 10 employees) shopping for group health plans through Gusto may face higher per-employee premiums than they would find through a dedicated insurance broker because Gusto's carrier network is narrower and does not include all regional insurers that might offer more competitive rates. The third hidden cost is the opportunity cost of limited HR capabilities — companies that outgrow Gusto's basic PTO tracking, time clock, or reporting features face a migration to a more robust HR platform (BambooHR, Rippling), which requires data export, employee retraining, and dual-system operation during transition. Gusto does not offer a native HRIS import/export for competitors, making migrations more expensive than they should be.

content.title: Learning Curve
content.body: Gusto has the shortest learning curve of any payroll platform, rated 9/10 for ease of use. Basic proficiency — running a payroll cycle, adding an employee, viewing payroll reports, and downloading tax documents — takes 30-60 minutes for most administrators. The platform's onboarding wizard guides new users through their first payroll run step by step, including employee setup, pay schedule configuration, and tax registration. Intermediate proficiency — configuring PTO policies, setting up benefits plans, managing contractor payments, creating custom reports, and integrating with QuickBooks or Xero — requires 2-4 hours of exploration spread over 1-2 payroll cycles. Advanced proficiency — handling complex multi-state payroll (employees working in multiple states), setting up garnishment deductions, configuring custom deduction types (parking, transit, charitable contributions), and managing ACA reporting — takes 1-2 weeks of hands-on experience typically covering two payroll runs. Gusto's employee portal requires no training at all — employees can view pay stubs, request time off, and update their direct deposit information without any instruction. The mobile app mirrors the web experience and is equally intuitive. Most admin training is self-guided through Gusto's knowledge base, which includes step-by-step articles with screenshots and video walkthroughs.

content.title: Setup Time
content.body: Gusto can be fully operational for most small businesses in 2-5 business days from account creation to first payroll run. Day one involves creating the Gusto account, entering company information (EIN, address, banking details), and setting up payroll preferences (pay schedules, overtime rules, deduction types) — approximately 30-60 minutes. Day two involves adding employee information including names, addresses, pay rates, withholding allowances, and bank account details for direct deposit — approximately 5-10 minutes per employee. Day three involves Gusto's tax registration process: Gusto registers the business with federal, state, and local tax agencies, which takes 1-3 business days depending on the agency's processing time. Simultaneously, Gusto initiates employee verification through the Department of Homeland Security's E-Verify system (for I-9 compliance). Day four or five involves running the first payroll, reviewing the payroll preview, and approving processing. Health benefits setup adds 1-2 weeks to the timeline, as it requires selecting plans from carriers, submitting applications, and waiting for carrier approval and group number assignment. Gusto provides onboarding specialists who handle the data migration from the previous payroll provider at no additional cost, including transferring year-to-date payroll data and historical tax filings.

content.title: Migration Difficulty
content.body: Migrating to Gusto from another payroll provider is rated 4/10 in difficulty — it is the easiest payroll migration in the market. Gusto's dedicated migration specialists handle most of the work: they coordinate with the previous provider to obtain payroll history, year-to-date tax filings, and employee data; register the business with tax agencies for Gusto processing; and validate that the first payroll matches expected amounts. The employer's responsibilities are limited to providing authorization for the previous provider to release data, entering employee bank account information for direct deposit, and reviewing the first payroll preview for accuracy. Migrating from ADP or Paychex to Gusto typically takes 2-3 weeks from initial contact to first payroll run. Migrating from manual payroll (spreadsheets or paper checks) is even faster at 1-2 weeks. The most complex part of the migration is multi-state tax registration — companies with employees in multiple states must ensure Gusto registers with each state's tax agency, and if any states have outstanding tax balances or unfiled returns, those must be resolved before Gusto can take over filing. Gusto handles year-to-date payroll data import, so W-2s and quarterly returns for the current year reflect the full year's payroll, not just Gusto-processed periods. Migrating away from Gusto is similarly straightforward: the platform provides downloadable employee data and payroll history in CSV format, and competitors' migration teams typically handle the data import.

content.title: Integration Ecosystem
content.body: Gusto's integration ecosystem focuses on the small business financial stack with 100+ integrations across accounting, time tracking, banking, and HR categories. The most impactful integrations are QuickBooks Online and Xero for accounting — Gusto provides two-way sync that posts payroll journal entries (wages, taxes, deductions, employer contributions) with mapped chart-of-accounts categories, eliminating manual data entry for bookkeeping. Time tracking integrations with TSheets, Homebase, When I Work, and Clockify import approved time entries directly into payroll hours, ensuring hourly employees are paid accurately without re-keying time data. Banking integrations with Mercury, Brex, Novo, and other small business banks enable direct deposit setup and automated funding from the integrated bank account. HR integrations include BambooHR and Rippling for companies that use Gusto specifically for payroll while managing other HR functions in separate platforms — these integrations sync employee data (new hires, terminations, compensation changes) bi-directionally to keep both systems up to date. Gusto also offers an API for custom integrations, though the API is more limited than platform-level APIs from competitors like Rippling. The integration ecosystem is well-curated for small business needs but lacks the depth that enterprises require — there are no native SIEM integrations, no advanced analytics connectors, and no workflow automation platform integration (Zapier integration is available but limited to triggers and actions on core employee events).

content.title: Security & Compliance
content.body: Gusto is SOC 2 Type II certified and uses bank-level 256-bit AES encryption for data at rest and TLS 1.3 for data in transit. Customer data is stored in logically separate environments with strict access controls, and Gusto maintains a formal security incident response program. Payroll processing is handled through Gusto's partnership with Stripe for payment processing, which provides additional PCI DSS Level 1 compliance coverage. Gusto's tax filing compliance is comprehensive: it handles federal, state, and local payroll tax calculations, filings, and payments across all 50 states and Washington D.C., including unemployment insurance, state income tax withholding, and local taxes where applicable (e.g., New York City, Philadelphia, San Francisco). The platform provides automatic compliance documentation generation including W-2s, 1095-Cs (ACA employer mandate), 1099-NECs, and I-9 forms with E-Verify integration. Gusto monitors regulatory changes and updates tax rates, form requirements, and compliance rules automatically — for example, when a state changes its unemployment tax rate, Gusto updates the rate in the platform without administrator action. For HIPAA compliance (relevant for benefit plan administration), Gusto signs Business Associate Agreements upon request. The platform does not offer customizable role-based access controls — all administrators have the same level of access, which is a limitation for companies that want tiered permission models.

content.title: Performance
content.body: Gusto's web application performs exceptionally well for a payroll platform, with page load times of 1-3 seconds for most views and payroll preview generation completing in 5-15 seconds even for companies with 100+ employees. The payroll processing itself takes approximately 2-4 hours once submitted — direct deposits are sent to the bank for processing, tax filings are submitted to the respective agencies, and payroll reports are generated in the background. Same-day direct deposit requests submitted before 7:00 AM PT are processed by 5:00 PM PT the same day. Tax filings are submitted on the schedule required by each agency — quarterly returns are filed by the regulatory deadline, and annual W-2s and 1099s are generated and distributed by January 31. Gusto's mobile app performs well for lightweight tasks — running payroll, viewing pay stubs, approving time-off requests, and checking PTO balances — though the app does not support full configuration or reporting. The platform has experienced rare but notable outages during high-traffic periods (end of quarter, end of year), with two reported service disruptions in 2025 lasting 2-4 hours each during which payroll could not be processed. Gusto's status page does not provide historical uptime data. Employee self-service is consistently responsive, with pay stub generation completing in under 2 seconds for 95% of requests.

content.title: Customer Support
content.body: Gusto provides customer support through email, phone, and chat with hours covering 6 AM to 6 PM PT Monday through Friday. All plans include access to Gusto's support team, but response time and phone support availability vary by plan. Simple plan customers primarily use email support with typical response times of 4-8 hours during business hours. Plus plan customers get priority email response (1-2 hours) and phone support for payroll-related issues. Premium plan customers receive a dedicated HR advisor (not a support agent — a credentialed HR professional who provides strategic guidance) and 24/7 phone support for critical issues. Support quality is rated 4.5/5 on G2 with consistently positive sentiment about the friendliness and helpfulness of support staff. Common praise includes the support team's willingness to handle complex tax registration issues on the customer's behalf (contacting state tax agencies directly) and the proactive follow-up after payroll processing issues. Common complaints include limited weekend support hours (no Saturday or Sunday support even during peak end-of-month payroll processing) and the lack of a live chat option for Simple and Plus plan customers — the chat feature is only available on the Premium plan. Gusto's self-service knowledge base is well-organized with searchable articles, video tutorials, and step-by-step guides for every feature. The knowledge base is integrated into the product's contextual help system, showing relevant articles based on the page the administrator is viewing.

content.title: Real-world Use Cases
content.body: A marketing agency with 15 full-time employees and 10 regular contractors uses Gusto Simple to run bi-weekly payroll, handle quarterly tax filings, and issue 1099-NECs to contractors at year end. The owner spends 15 minutes per payroll run reviewing the preview and approving processing. Automated tax filing eliminated the quarterly anxiety of calculating and filing payroll taxes, and the 100% accuracy guarantee provides peace of mind. A 30-person architecture firm uses Gusto Plus with health benefits administration: they offer two medical plans (PPO and HDHP with HSA), dental, and vision coverage through Gusto's benefits marketplace. Open enrollment is managed entirely through the platform, with employees selecting plans and electing coverage amounts and Gusto syncing premium deductions directly to payroll. The firm's office manager estimates that benefits administration through Gusto saves 8-10 hours per month compared to their previous manual process. A 50-person e-commerce company uses Gusto Premium with the dedicated HR advisor to handle complex HR situations including employee performance issues, termination compliance, and state-specific leave law questions. The advisor provides guidance on California's paid sick leave requirements, New York's paid family leave, and multi-state overtime compliance. The company also uses Gusto's time tracking with GPS for their warehouse staff, with hours synced directly to payroll for accurate bi-weekly processing.

content.title: Common Mistakes
content.body: Five mistakes repeatedly surface from Gusto administrators. Setting incorrect pay schedules — selecting the wrong pay period frequency (weekly vs bi-weekly vs semi-monthly) during initial setup causes confusion throughout the year and requires a Gusto support case to change. Verify the pay schedule with state requirements before committing, as some states have specific pay frequency requirements. Not reviewing payroll previews carefully — the payroll preview screen shows calculated wages, taxes, and deductions before processing, but administrators who approve without reviewing miss errors in hourly time entries, incorrect overtime calculations, or missing deductions. Always verify the gross-to-net calculation for at least one salaried and one hourly employee every payroll run. Misclassifying employees as contractors — Gusto provides tools for classifying workers, but misclassification (treating an employee as a 1099 contractor) creates significant tax liability and legal exposure. Review the IRS 20-factor test and consult with a CPA before classifying workers. Neglecting state registrations for new hire states — when hiring an employee in a new state, Gusto must register the business with that state's tax agency before processing payroll. Starting payroll before registration is complete results in unfiled tax returns. Initiate state registration at least two weeks before the first payroll in that state. Ignoring PTO accrual configuration — setting up PTO policies without understanding accrual methods (accrual per pay period vs lump sum at hire vs anniversary date) creates confusion when employees see unexpected PTO balances. Model PTO scenarios for at least one new hire and one departing employee before activating policies.

content.title: Tips from experienced users
content.body: Experienced Gusto administrators recommend five practices. Set up contractor-only pay schedules separately from employee schedules — contractors are often paid on different cadences (upon milestone completion, net-30) than employees (bi-weekly), and separating pay schedules prevents confusion in payroll previews and reporting. Use Gusto's time tracking directly instead of integrating with third-party time clocks — the native time tracking pushes approved hours directly into the payroll preview, eliminating the troubleshooting step of verifying that third-party time data imported correctly. Gusto's GPS-enabled clock-in/out is sufficient for most small businesses. Configure custom deduction types (parking, transit benefits, charitable donations) before the first payroll of the year — adding deductions mid-year requires pro-rated calculations and can create discrepancies in year-to-date reporting. Test Gusto's off-cycle payroll feature before you need it — running a bonus payroll, correcting a missed pay period, or processing termination pay requires understanding the off-cycle workflow, which differs from regular payroll processing. Gusto support can walk through the process, but testing in a sandbox prevents errors during time-sensitive situations. Review Gusto's tax compliance dashboard monthly — the dashboard shows the status of all registered tax accounts, upcoming filing deadlines, and any compliance issues. A monthly review catches registration problems or unfiled returns before they trigger agency notices or penalties.

content.title: Alternatives
content.body: Gusto's primary competitors target different segments of the payroll and HR market. ADP Run is the direct small business competitor from the industry giant, offering comparable payroll automation with a broader range of add-on HR services (background checks, learning management, performance reviews) at competitive pricing for businesses over 25 employees — though ADP's interface is less intuitive than Gusto's and its per-employee pricing is less transparent. Paychex Flex similarly targets small to mid-sized businesses with more comprehensive HR features (succession planning, workforce planning, advanced reporting) and better support for multi-state and multi-entity companies, but with a lower ease-of-use rating and more aggressive upselling during implementation. Rippling offers a broader platform that combines payroll, HR, IT management, and device management in a unified system with global payroll capabilities — it is Gusto's most direct competitor for companies that want HR plus IT automation, though Rippling is more expensive and complex to set up than Gusto. BambooHR excels at HR features (performance management, time tracking, applicant tracking) but handles payroll through a partnership with Buncee (US) and local providers (international) rather than offering native payroll. QuickBooks Payroll is the cheapest option for existing QuickBooks users at $22.50/month plus $5/employee, but its tax filing and HR capabilities are significantly more limited than Gusto's. For each alternative, the migration effort is low (Gusto provides data export), but the cost of retraining administrators on a new platform and the risk of payroll processing errors during transition favor staying with Gusto for its ease of use.

content.title: Final Verdict
content.body: Gusto earns a 4.5/5 rating as the best payroll and HR platform for US-based small businesses with 1-100 employees. Its consumer-grade user experience, automated tax filing with accuracy guarantee, and integrated benefits administration make it the clear leader in ease of use and small business accessibility. The platform is not the most cost-effective option at scale (ADP and Paychex offer better per-employee pricing above 25 employees), does not support international payroll, and lacks the advanced HR features that companies need as they grow beyond 100 employees. Gusto's best-fit customer is a 5-25 person US company that wants to spend 15 minutes per payroll run instead of 2 hours, and that values automated compliance over platform breadth. The dedicated HR advisor on the Premium plan is a genuinely valuable differentiator for companies that need strategic HR guidance without hiring an in-house HR person. Buy it for the user experience and tax automation; plan to evaluate Rippling or ADP when headcount exceeds 50 or when international hiring becomes necessary.

faqs.question: How does Gusto handle tax filing and what happens if there is an error?
faqs.answer: Gusto automatically calculates payroll taxes for every run, files them with the appropriate federal, state, and local agencies on the required schedule, and withdraws the funds from your linked bank account to pay them. The platform handles all quarterly and year-end filings including Form 941, state unemployment tax, and W-2s. Gusto guarantees 100% accuracy for all tax calculations and filings — if they make a mistake, they will pay any resulting penalties and interest. This guarantee is a significant selling point for small business owners who dread tax paperwork.

faqs.question: Can I run payroll for salaried and hourly employees on different schedules?
faqs.answer: Yes, Gusto supports multiple pay schedules simultaneously. You can run monthly payroll for salaried employees while processing weekly or bi-weekly payroll for hourly workers. The platform tracks each employee's pay schedule, calculates overtime automatically based on their hours, and handles accruals for PTO and sick leave. You can also run off-cycle payrolls for bonuses, commissions, or corrections without disrupting the regular schedule.

faqs.question: Does Gusto offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Gusto does not offer a free trial, but it provides a free demo and a 30-day money-back guarantee on your first payroll run if you are not satisfied. The company also offers a free Employer Account that gives you access to state and federal compliance alerts, document templates, and the ability to preview pricing without entering billing details.

faqs.question: How does Gusto compare to ADP and Paychex for small businesses?
faqs.answer: Gusto is significantly easier to use than both ADP and Paychex, with a modern interface that requires minimal training. ADP and Paychex offer more comprehensive features for large enterprises but their small business packages feel like scaled-down versions of complex enterprise software. Gusto is built ground-up for small businesses, which is evident in its onboarding flow, employee portal, and transparent pricing.

author: StackPilot Team
